steps.title: Identify the key principle
steps.content: The strongest clue is Defoe's repeated instruction: when a story has “sufficient authority,” historians must credit “its proper voucher”; when it lacks authority, they must “say so.” Both actions center on the same principle—transparency about source credibility.

steps.title: Match to the answer
steps.content: Choice D states that historians should “clearly indicate the extent to which each of their sources is trustworthy.” This captures Defoe's dual requirement: making source credibility transparent in both strong and weak cases. The others mischaracterize: A claims records are false rather than unverified; B restricts historians to credible sources only; C invents unsupported remorse. That matches choice D — D is correct.
